commented_code:
If set to :const:`None` or 0 the connection pool will be disabled and
 connections will be established and closed for every use.
 
+.. setting:: broker_pool_acquire_timeout
+
+``broker_pool_acquire_timeout``
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
+
+.. versionadded:: 5.7
+
+Default: :const:`None` (block indefinitely).
+
+The maximum number of seconds to wait when acquiring a connection or producer
+from the broker pool. When all :setting:`broker_pool_limit` connections are in
+use, ``pool.acquire`` will block up to this many seconds before raising
+:exc:`~celery.exceptions.OperationalError`.
+
+Set this to a positive number (e.g. ``120``) to prevent
+``send_task`` / ``apply_async`` calls from blocking indefinitely under
+high concurrency. When :const:`None`, the previous behavior of blocking
+without a timeout is preserved.

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -3115,6 +3116,25 @@ contention can arise and you should consider increasing the limit.
 If set to :const:`None` or 0 the connection pool will be disabled and
 connections will be established and closed for every use.
 
+.. setting:: broker_pool_acquire_timeout
+
+``broker_pool_acquire_timeout``
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
+
+.. versionadded:: 5.7
+
+Default: :const:`None` (block indefinitely).
+
+The maximum number of seconds to wait when acquiring a connection or producer
+from the broker pool. When all :setting:`broker_pool_limit` connections are in
+use, ``pool.acquire`` will block up to this many seconds before raising
+:exc:`~celery.exceptions.OperationalError`.
+
+Set this to a positive number (e.g. ``120``) to prevent
+``send_task`` / ``apply_async`` calls from blocking indefinitely under
+high concurrency. When :const:`None`, the previous behavior of blocking
+without a timeout is preserved.
discussion_comments.comment_body:
Fair point on precision. The current wording is accurate for the intended scope — `send_task`/`apply_async` are the high-level APIs that go through `producer_or_acquire()` and `connection_or_acquire()`, which is where the timeout is enforced. The two other call sites (`events.py`, `rpc.py`) are worker-internal paths with different contention characteristics.

Happy to tighten the wording if the maintainers prefer, but the current description matches the documented scope: preventing client-side `send_task`/`apply_async` from blocking indefinitely.

commented_code:
$ sudo rabbitmqctl stop
 
 When the server is running, you can continue reading `Setting up RabbitMQ`_.
+
+.. _using-quorum-queues:
+
+Using Quorum Queues
+===================
+
+.. warning::
+
+    Quorum Queues require disabling global QoS which means some features won't work as expected.
+    See `limitations`_ for details.
+
+Celery supports `Quorum Queues`_ by setting the ``x-queue-type`` header to ``quorum` like so:
+
+.. code-block:: python
+
+    from kombu import Queue
+
+    task_queues = [Queue('my-queue', queue_arguments={'x-queue-type': 'quorum'})]
+    broker_transport_options = {"confirm_publish": True}
+
+If you'd like to change the type of the default queue, set the :setting:`task_default_queue_type` setting to ``quorum``.
+
+Celery automatically detects if quorum queues are used using the :setting:`worker_detect_quorum_queues` setting.
+We recommend to keep the default behavior turned on.
+
+.. _`Quorum Queues`: https://www.rabbitmq.com/docs/quorum-queues
+
+.. _limitations:
+
+Limitations
+-----------
+
+Disabling global QoS means that the the per-channel QoS is now static.
+This means that some Celery features won't work when using Quorum Queues.
+
+Autoscaling relies on increasing and decreasing the prefetch count whenever a new process is instantiated
+or terminated so it won't work when Quorum Queues are detected.
+
+Similarly, the :setting:`worker_enable_prefetch_count_reduction` setting will be a no-op even when set to ``True``
+when Quorum Queues are detected.
+
+In addition, :ref:`ETA/Countdown <calling-eta>` will block the worker when received until the ETA arrives since
+we can no longer increase the prefetch count and fetch another task from the queue.
+
+In order to properly schedule ETA/Countdown tasks you need to enable :ref:`Native Delayed Delivery <native-delayed-delivery>`.
+
+.. _native-delayed-delivery:
+
+Native Delayed Delivery
+-----------------------
+
+Since tasks with ETA/Countdown will block the worker until they are scheduled for execution,
+we need to use RabbitMQ's native capabilities to schedule the execution of tasks.
+
+The design is borrowed from NServiceBus. If you are interested in the implementation details, refer to their `documentation`_.
+
+.. _documentation: https://docs.particular.net/transports/rabbitmq/delayed-delivery
+
+To enable Native Delayed Delivery set the :setting:`broker_native_delayed_delivery` setting to ``True``.

Again, all mentions of the flag need to be adjusted. It has to be automatic, or else the design will be error-prone and will cause either headaches or waste a lot of time reporting and investigating issues.

**People will miss this flag, trust me bro.** @thedrow

discussion_comments.comment_body:
So the way I see it,
Option 1) Log warning and expect user to see, read, understand, look for doc and adjust/fix code, even if it’s a simple line or two of change.
Option 2) No warning, automatic detection of state and automatic configuration accordingly. The user knows nothing, needs to configure nothing, and the behavior with/without quorum queues is the same from the POV the user/API, which is the definition of ETA support for quorum queues. Requiring to enable more flags creates a design dependency which affect the readability of the feature, breaks compatibility with the standard ETA feature (which doesn’t requires any flags) and by nature and definition, is error-prone (automatic/manual approach).

How come Option 2 doesn’t appeal to you @thedrow ?

discussion_comments.comment_body:
> Because my client wants to test this feature without using quorum queues at first.

This is not a logical design argument. If your client doesn’t trust the feature, you and your client need to refine your delivery process. The ETA mechanism should be enabled automatically when the conditions are detected automatically. If anyone wants to disable the mechanism due to a lack of trust or pragmatic feature migration, the responsibility for configuring the feature is on the user, per the specific needs/tests/migration phase that is relevant at the moment.

> I think we can assume developers see this warning and that's just fine by me.

This is an error-prone design by definition, which lowers the quality of the mechanism as a whole; and this is not fine by me.


> So the way I see it, Option 1) Log warning and expect user to see, read, understand, look for doc and adjust/fix code, even if it’s a simple line or two of change. Option 2) No warning, automatic detection of state and automatic configuration accordingly. The user knows nothing, needs to configure nothing, and the behavior with/without quorum queues is the same from the POV the user/API, which is the definition of ETA support for quorum queues. Requiring to enable more flags creates a design dependency which affect the readability of the feature, breaks compatibility with the standard ETA feature (which doesn’t requires any flags) and by nature and definition, is error-prone (automatic/manual approach).
> 
> How come Option 2 doesn’t appeal to you @thedrow ?

I asked for **your** professional opinion @thedrow; do you support the first or the second design options I proposed?
If you support the first option, please explain why is it better for Celery over design option number 2, because I can’t see a stronger argument than “requiring the client to do nothing, it is all smartly and automatically detected and configured by itself”.

P.S
The second option is also better for your client, as it enables pragmatic migration without forcing custom requirements into a general and such a core feature design.

discussion_comments.comment_body: Native delayed delivery is now enabled automatically when quorum queues are detected.
We can go all the way back to `pip install "tzlocal==0.2"` and it still works.
i think this is added on bdist rpm. instead this should be added to somewhere it is not platform specific

I added the `tzlocal` dependency in two places:

1. `requirements/default.txt`

```
billiard>=4.2.1,<5.0
kombu>=5.6.0b2,<5.7
vine>=5.1.0,<6.0
click>=8.1.2,<9.0
click-didyoumean>=0.3.0
click-repl>=0.2.0
click-plugins>=1.1.1
backports.zoneinfo[tzdata]>=0.2.1; python_version < '3.9'
python-dateutil>=2.8.2
tzlocal
```

2. `setup.cfg`

```
[bdist_rpm]
requires = backports.zoneinfo>=0.2.1;python_version<'3.9'
           tzdata>=2022.7
           tzlocal
           billiard >=4.1.0,<5.0
           kombu >= 5.3.4,<6.0.0
```

So some packages are in both and some are not. Some like `billiard` and `kombu` don't have matching version.

tzlocal does exist for Ubuntu:

```
apt show python3-tzlocal

Package: python3-tzlocal
Version: 5.2-1.1
Priority: optional
Section: universe/python
Source: python-tzlocal
Origin: Ubuntu
Maintainer: Ubuntu Developers <ubuntu-devel-discuss@lists.ubuntu.com>
Original-Maintainer: Debian Python Team <team+python@tracker.debian.org>
Bugs: https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+filebug
Installed-Size: 86.0 kB
Depends: python3:any
Homepage: https://github.com/regebro/tzlocal
Download-Size: 20.5 kB
APT-Sources: http://ubuntu.mirrors.ovh.net/ubuntu noble/universe amd64 Packages
Description: tzinfo object for the local timezone
 This Python module returns a tzinfo object with the local timezone
 information.
 .
 Example of usage:
 .
    >>> from tzlocal import get_localzone
    >>> tz = get_localzone()
    >>> tz
    <DstTzInfo 'Europe/Warsaw' WMT+1:24:00 STD>
```

Anyway, long story short: do you want to me to remove `tzlocal` from `[bdist_rpm]` in `setup.cfg`?

I would expect everything in `requirements/default.txt` to also be in `[bdist_rpm]` though. How else is the RPM build supposed to be usable?
